task: I got a bill from a contractor that looks a little off, and I don’t want to just pay it without checking. What’s the smartest way to review it and push back if something doesn’t add up?

turns.agent_response:
Smartest approach: **review it like a punch list, not like a favor**.

### 1) Compare the bill to the original written agreement
Check that the invoice matches:
- exact scope of work
- agreed price
- deposit schedule
- materials specified
- start date / completion date
- permit responsibility
- warranty terms
- cleanup/damage responsibility
- any approved change orders

If anything is missing or different, flag it.

### 2) Verify the job was actually completed
Before paying, do a full inspection:
- run the system yourself
- check for leaks, gaps, caulk, paint, scratches, loose fittings
- confirm the materials used match what was promised
- test plumbing/electrical twice if relevant
- check cleanup and debris removal

### 3) Look for common billing red flags
- charges for work not authorized
- duplicate line items
- “extra” labor with no written approval
- material markups that weren’t agreed to
- vague descriptions like “miscellaneous”
- billing for unfinished or defective work

### 4) Hold payment if there’s a problem
If something doesn’t add up:
- pay only the undisputed amount, if appropriate
- withhold the rest until resolved
- create a short punch list of issues
- ask for a corrected invoice in writing

### 5) Push back in writing, briefly and firmly
Use something like:

> I reviewed the invoice against our agreement and found a few items that don’t match the approved scope/price. Please send a corrected invoice and written explanation for the disputed charges. I’ll hold payment on the disputed portion until the punch list is resolved.

### 6) Keep your evidence organized
Save:
- contract
- texts/emails
- photos/video
- invoice
- change orders
- notes from your inspection

If you want, I can help you make a **one-page invoice review checklist** or draft a **pushback email** based on the bill.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right shape of it. Short, firm, and in writing.

I’d do it in this order:
- pull the estimate and any change orders
- mark every line on the bill against what was actually agreed to
- check the work myself before a dime leaves my hand
- separate the disputed part from the part that’s plain enough to pay
- keep copies of everything, not just some of it

If they start getting slippery, I’d want the corrected invoice, not a story.

The wording you gave is fine. Maybe a little cleaner:

“I reviewed the invoice against our agreement. A few charges do not match the approved scope and price. Send a corrected invoice and a written explanation for the disputed items. I will hold payment on the disputed portion until the issues are resolved.”

That’s plenty. No speeches. No back-and-forth on the phone if you can avoid it.
